item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a fantastic article, and the heights and depths of emotion are conveyed beautifully and feel earned, rather than cloying or exploitative. I do have to say that the mother's dialogue as written goes way overboard with the eye dialect, to me, making it comically stereotypical and borderline offensive. I really think that could be dialed back, but the rest of it is just top-notch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Someone gets to live in the light, almost, for once. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They're both stories steeped in personal tragedy and a broken family, and how a fracture can get infected. Like I said, I think this one is less unique and less ambitious, but I get more enjoyment out of the story its telling and I like the characters more. 2740 was more an experiment in dreamlike Groundhogs Day horror while this was a character piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a huge fan of 2740!
…but what caused you to draw that comparison, if I may ask? :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That part didn't bug me as much. The anomaly was incorporated better in 2740, but this story resonated with me more. Since it's a story first and foremost, and I enjoyed the story and the characters, I upvoted. It's a tad on the generic side, but the characterization was really strong and I got a good sense of who these characters are and what was going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You said "magic fire little girl" in the chat, and friend, after seeing that phrasing, I was admittedly very nervous about opening this response you penned. (especially since another individual had basically previously commented about how one of my concerns about the piece was legitimate/expressed dislike)

(anyways. Digressing already. Woke up after midnight from sleep paralysis, don't mind me)

I was trying to achieve a couple of things with this piece.

1. I didn't know how to articulate it at first, but a Reddit user helped. I wanted a 'Pet the Dog' /'morality pet' situation for the Foundation. they just…Foundation folk have a thankless job, like I mentioned earlier in the thread. My little Dierdre needed a better life. Even the Foundation would be preferable to what she had. She can have a normal childhood, now. 🌈

2. I wanted a happy ending for at least one child stuck inside these nightmare situations. Her house was a broken home, so she burned it down. She doesn't need to make fires anymore, either. She's risen from the ashes and soared off into the new dawn. Even if it's from behind the bars of a cage.

Thank you so much for reading and commenting. Thank you thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm usually not found of stories like this involving these themes1 because they're generally just forced drama but this has two distinct differences:

  • These all seem like things that could happen without the excuse of "It needs to be sad.".
  • Everybody is characterized very well.

Primarily how despite going through some fucked up shit SCP-3474 herself still has a negative view on vigilante justice and makes some level of recovery making her seem like a legitimate person to care about2 rather than a punching bag for a sob story.

Another important thing is Suzannah and how she's not really good either. You did a very good job capturing the delusional mental state of a drug addict that dosn't even seem to fully register that she is basically destroying her daughter's life, Making an interesting contrast to SCP-3474 who is extremely guilty about things that don't really even make sense in context. And then it ends perfectly when the mother runs out of solutions she resorts to just killing the guy rather than, idk, not taking the shit. It's very realistic to a disturbing level how she doesn't even seem to consider getting clean and decides that murder is the more rational solution.

Footnotes
1. drunk father figure abuse either lazy or otherwise unhelpful mom etc.
2. Particularly that line about how he made her "sit on his lap" was a pretty big oh shit moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i can't bring myself to upvote this honestly.

i feel like stories with little girls almost always revolve around some form of sexual trauma/abuse (here implied), and here that doesn't work because it means the anomaly doesn't even come into the frame. the whole narrative here is focused on the (admittedly terrible and heart-wrenching) experiences of these two women, and the anomaly completely fades into the background. this story almost reads no differently than if the foundation were the local police and the little girl had instead just stabbed the boyfriend to death.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So my very first draft was the one that was technically correct. That's basically what I had, until others suggested the opposite.

Agh.

I appreciate the feedback. Learning experience for the next one, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
thus, it's really only used to describe D-class personnel.

Or any other expiremental subject including lab animals, or mineral samples, or even civilian populations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've never seen anyone with site experience tell someone to refer to an SCP object as 'subject', and have only heard the opposite, which is how I learned it and started enforcing it. 'Subject' is reserved for the subject of whatever given test, thus, it's really only used to describe D-class personnel. EDIT: Not that there aren't other cases of use, like sirpudding said, but D-class seems to be the most common application in SCP articles.

Typically it's encouraged to use "the object" or "the anomaly" or "the entity" depending on what sounds the best for the type of anomaly it is. I don't see specimen used a lot but I also don't see anything tonally wrong with it. For this skip, a human, they'd probably try pretty hard not to use object (especially since it's a kid and they're trying to be generally less shitty in terms of ethics) but would still call it the other ones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agh, I missed that bit in my final edit. Meant to replace all of the redacted stuff with what actually was there. Appreciate the catch.

Also, the use of "the subject"…I just feel like I can't win with that bit. I've had multiple editors pulling me in different directions on what to use. I was told "subject" by one person, "specimen" by another, "object" by yet another…you can imagine my frustration in that regard. What would be your suggestion, to avoid abrasive vocabulary in the future?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's good, and I like it overall, but the outdated use of 'subject' to denote the anomaly was rampant and it became pretty grating. That's pretty much my only issue, other than this:

In addition to the head trauma, Karl Homlich received injuries to [DATA EXPUNGED]. This seems highly personal, Mrs. Phillips.

I really don't think they'd expunge "his penis" or "his groin" in this context. This is a conversation about two people fighting and there's nothing explicit enough here to warrant removal from a formal report.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you so very, very much!

I wanted a happy ending for all involved. (well, mostly all) And Foundation work is such a thankless job, yaknow? I wanted to give at least some staff members the chance to be a little human :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something turns out in a way that does not rend my heart.

+1
